data.title: PTY: Efficient Portfolio Strategy Results In Outperformance Against Peers
data.description:
Overview

As interest rates remain high, there is a lot of uncertainty within the debt markets. However, there are plenty of high-quality opportunities out there that investors can take advantage of. I believe that PIMCO Corporate and Income Opportunity Fund (PTY) is one of the best opportunities in the high-yield debt investment sector right now, due to the attractive valuation and conservative risk profile. When I previously covered PTY, I issued a buy rating due to the potential for growth as interest rates decline.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PTY's share price has declined by about 10.8%. This fund has continued to trend downward since the momentum shift in mid-October. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits at a loss of 1.1% over the same time frame. Following the pullback, PTY now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 10.8% and issues payouts on a monthly basis, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking a source of reliable income generation.

Since my last coverage, PTY's valuation remains attractive and justifies the accumulation of shares. However, I also wanted to revisit the fund to discuss how it is one of the best-performing PIMCO funds at this time. Compared to relevant peers, PTY's portfolio strategy utilizes a reasonable amount of leverage that has led to higher returns. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that PTY implements to generate its earnings for shareholders.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's latest overview, PTY has total managed assets of $2.96B that are spread across a diverse range of income producing securities. The fund implements a dynamic strategy that spreads its assets across securities with varying duration, credit quality, and sectors. Under normal market conditions, PTY aims to invest at least 80% of its assets into a combination of debt obligations and other corporate income producing securities. The fund leans most heavily on high-yield credit, but the full breakdown of the portfolio is as follows:

  • High Yield Credit: 37.96%
  • Non-USD Developed: 15.12%
  • Emerging Markets: 13.67%
  • Non-Agency Mortgage: 13.41%
  • Investment Grade Credit: 6.73%
  • Other: 4.78%
  • US Government Related: 4.12%
  • CMBS: 1.6%

Expanding on this diversity, PTY also spreads its assets across a diverse range of industries to help avoid any sort of concentration risk to any one specific area. As we can see below, PTY is most heavily allocated to Technology investments at 12.91% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Media Cable and Healthcare securities, which accounts for 6.07% and 5.02% of assets, respectively. While the fund leans most heavily to U.S.-based securities, PTY also provides a bit of exposure to global securities spanning across France, Spain, Brazil, Germany, and more.

Since PTY is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, PTY now trades at a premium to NAV of 10.75%. For reference, PTY has traded at an average premium to NAV of 23.1% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that PTY currently trades at one of the most favorable price-to-NAV levels over the last decade, which may now justify the accumulation of shares.

Performance Risk & Comparison

Despite the fund's diverse exposure, investors need to remember that PTY prioritizes income generation above all else. PTY holds its securities through different income securities rather than common equity. To demonstrate this vulnerability, we can refer to the performance chart over the last ten year period below. As we can see, PTY's share price has declined by about 4.4% over the last ten years, which makes it clear that PTY has missed out on many of the market rallies over the last decade. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 161% over the same time. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of the returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

Additionally, PTY utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify returns over time. The use of leverage is captured through the combination of reverse repurchase agreements and credit default swaps, which amounts to 18.19% of total assets. I consider this to be a very moderate use of leverage, which shouldn't significantly increase the risk profile of the fund. However, leverage still has some impact on the fund's operating spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from its securities.

So despite the Fed initiating its second 25 basis point cut to interest rates, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten year range. As we can see below, this higher interest rate environment has contributed to PTY's share price remaining suppressed in recent years. I anticipate that PTY's share price may eventually maintain an uptrend once interest rates continue to trend downward.

PIMCO has a plethora of funds under their umbrella that its investors can choose from. While their underlying strategy in terms of sector focus, security type, and risk profile may differ, the underlying premise of generating income from a pool of debt securities remains the same. I believe that the two most compelling peers for investors to choose from are the following:

  • PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI): ~14.6% dividend yield.
  • PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund (PCN): ~10.7% dividend yield.

As we can see below, PCN has the longest operating history, with an inception dating back to 2001, but this fund has the smallest assets under management at $848M. Conversely, PDI has the shortest operating history but a much larger base of assets under management at $7.2B. PCN is probably the most similar to PTY, because the underlying portfolio strategy also focuses on high yield credit securities and other income producing securities. Conversely, PDI leans more heavily on mortgage-related securities to generate its earnings.

When comparing the performance of the peer group, PTY outperforms these alternatives by a wide margin of performance. Over the last ten years, PTY's total return sits above 161%, which outpaces the total return of PDI and PCN over the same time frame. The outperformance can be attributed to the breakdown of securities by credit rating and the different use of leverage. PTY's leverage is quite moderate, whereas PDI's use of leverage is much higher, while PCN uses a very little amount of leverage to amplify its earnings. Therefore, I believe PTY is the most appealing choice for long-term investors out of this peer group.

The standout feature of PTY is that its breakdown by credit ratings differs from its peers. PTY allocates a larger percentage of its assets towards securities with higher credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating of BBB and above indicate strength and reduce the chance that PTY is impacted by defaults. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 57.6%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 33.7%
  • Not Rated: 8.71%

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1188 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 10.8%. Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that PTY has maintained a long consistency of steady payouts over the last decade. The distributions were cut back in mid-2021, but this may be attributed to the uncertainty of the pandemic at the time. Additionally, the Fed cut interest rates to near zero levels, so the income received from a portion of PTY's investments may have decreased. Despite these challenges, the fund has kept payouts within a tight range, which makes PTY a great option for investors seeking a reliable source of dividend income.

Unfortunately, I wouldn't anticipate that PTY will provide any sort of traditional dividend growth over time. However, the fund is already providing a double-digit yield, so investors should limit their expectations around dividend growth. The good thing about the consistent payouts is that this allows investors to create their own dividend growth by reinvesting distributions or adding new capital to their position over time.

However, investors should consider the fact that the distributions received from PTY do not offer tax efficiency. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 73.14% of the distribution has been paid out using net investment income, which is classified as ordinary dividends and has the highest tax burden on investors. Therefore, a position in PTY would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset any of the potential tax consequences.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on PTY due to the attractive valuation and efficient portfolio strategy. The fund now trades at a favorable premium to NAV valuation, relative to its historical range. Additionally, PTY's portfolio strategy of focusing on higher-rated securities while utilizing a moderate amount of leverage has resulted in outperformance against other PIMCO peers. In a scenario where interest rates continue to trend downward, PTY's share price may trend upward as the operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities may improve.

data.title: Canada Labor Market Watch: Unemployment Rate Drops To 6.5%
data.description:
Canada’s unemployment rate dropped substantially and unexpectedly to 6.5% in November, from 6.9% in the prior month, showing signs of a much-needed resiliency in the North American economy.

Latest Data

Metric November 2025 Prior Month (Oct 2025)
Unemployment Rate 6.5% 6.9%
Total Employment Change +53,600 +66,600
Part-Time Employment Change +63,000 +85,100
Full-Time Employment Change -9,400 -18,500
Job Finding Rate 19.6% 19.8%

Surprise labour market recovery

A 40 basis point (bps) drop in the unemployment rate is substantial. An improvement of this magnitude was last seen in November 2005, outside the period of COVID distortions seen between 2020 and mid-2022.

The strengthening of the labor market is best illustrated by the employment growth readings, which now show three consecutive months of job growth. In November, Canada recorded a gain of 54,000 jobs, following increases of 60,400 and 66,600 in September and October, respectively.

The Part-Time/Full-Time Divergence

However, the details offered some caution. For two months in a row all the employment increases came from part-time jobs (63,000 in November and 85,100 in October). If we look at full-time jobs, a combined 27,900 jobs were lost in the last two months.

The StatCan in its press release, provided some context to ease some of the worries that the recent surge in part-time employment is a sign of economic distress. The percentage of part-time employment deemed “involuntary,” defined as the decision to work part-time due to economic or business conditions or an inability to find full-time work, was 17.9% in November.

  • That is just slightly higher than the 17.6% reading in Nov 2024; and,
  • lower than the average for the Novembers from 2017 to 2019 (19.3%).

Another important figure StatCan emphasized is the job-finding rate, which was 19.6% in November, which was higher than the 18.6% a year earlier and similar to the figure reported in September (19.8%).

Health care is the biggest job creator

In terms of industry breakdown, health care and social assistance is the biggest job creator in November, adding 45,500 jobs in the month, a rebound from October’s 7,200 drop.

Wholesale and retail trade, on the other hand, lost 34,100 jobs after it gained 40,700 in the previous month. Manufacturing also cut 9,300 workers after a combined 36,500 job creation in the previous two months.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Oscar Health: Banking On The ACA Enrollment Expansion (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

I am reviewing my previous hold rating on Oscar Health, Inc. (OSCR) to a buy rating driven by recent Affordable Care Act (ACA) enrollment expansion to Alabama and Southern Florida, among other areas. This expansion shows foresight in scaling membership while the economics around health insurance remain tight. In my previous article, I stated that amid steep insurance budget cuts, the total projected membership by 2027 was around 20 million members. Following the ACA enrollment expansion, the projected membership has now gone up to 24 million subscribers without subsidies and around 31 million subscribers with subsidies.

Already, the current enrollment outlook is without subsidies in the picture, after the deal ending the U.S.'s most extended shutdown. The deal ending the unprecedented shutdown in the history of the U.S. did not include an extension to tax credits that cushion the cost of accessing ACA Marketplaces. Despite this hiccup, the expansion seems to be the driver of the optimism of the YTD price return of 26.93%, beating the S&P 500 price return at 15.83%.

I will dive deeper into ACA enrollment expansion, how it positions the company, and why this optimism warrants a bullish outlook.

ACA Enrollment Expansion Growth Outlook

In my previous article, I highlighted the looming uncertainty from healthcare budget cuts in 2026, which is about 30 days away. From what I see, this uncertainty is even clearer given the shutdown deal that did not provide a clear path to the expiring federal tax credit that offsets some costs on ACA plans. The ACA tax credits/subsidies are set to end by 2025, which means this may affect the number of subscribers under the Advance Premium Tax Credit (APTC). This is because the ACA is directly tied to APTC, and the failure to extend these benefits after the shutdown for the year 2026 will result in lowered premiums, which will call for subscribers to go out-of-pocket to meet these costs.

Over the years, the ACA enrollment has been increasing significantly due to subsidies. For example, the current subscribers under APTC stand at around 22.4 million, from 19.7 million in 2024, representing a 13.7% increase. Compared to the number of consumers without APTC, which stands at 1.9 million, compared to 1.7 million consumers in 2024, this is a small percentage of the total consumers. There is growth, but it indicates just how the subsidies are key to ACA enrollment.

Now, when I look at the difference between consumers with APTC, it is easier to understand why it has been a primary issue resulting in the longest shutdown in the history of the U.S. As such, President Donald Trump has expressed intentions of extending ACA subsidies, terming them as necessary to cushion Americans facing their premiums skyrocketing. It is also rumored that even though ACA subsidies are set to end by 31st December 2025, lawmakers are looking at 30th January 2026 as the real end of healthcare subsidies. From where I sit, my view is that since there could be another shutdown after 30th January 2026 due to an end to healthcare subsidies, there are high chances of an extension of subsidies for three more years, even if it might require some Americans to dig deeper into their pockets.

From these external headwinds facing ACA enrollment, let’s get to Oscar Health. Despite these uncertainties, Oscar Health is still extending its reach, and from what I see, Oscar is best positioned amidst this uncertainty. In a scenario without subsidies, the company projects to have access to a total market size enrollment of around 24 million members by 2027 and 31 million members with subsidies during the same year.

This is why the expansion is crucial at this uncertainty; even without subsidies, Oscar Health has a chance to stay afloat in membership due to expansion into new metropolitan regions and innovative offerings such as small-group and ICHRA coverage. The areas like Arizona, San Antonio, New Jersey, Alabama, among others, have expanded to have a high enrollment percentage of between 100% and 200% in 5 years.

For example, in Alabama, which is within 100% to 200%, has recorded enrollment growth of 198%, from 160,429 members in 2020 to 477,838 members in 2025. Therefore, by extending to regions recording high enrollment percentages, this positions Oscar Health for more enrollment opportunities from these areas, offering ICHRA and small-group coverage. Still, taking Alabama as my pragmatic example, Alabama is also expecting to see a rise in insurance costs, which could double. Alabama Public Education Employees Health Insurance Plan (PEEHIP) is planning to request a raise of around $380 million from the legislature in an attempt to cushion employees’ plan coverage for over 104,000 people covered under its scheme.

Such actions are positioning Oscar Health to capture more memberships with its affordable health plans, such as an annual bronze deductible, which is $4,548, way cheaper than the average, which is around $5,523. On average, a 21-year-old is paying for monthly health insurance of $445, and compared to Oscar Health at $410, expansion to new areas expands market reach for potential customers in need of affordable coverage.

Financials and Valuation

The revenue performance is also one of the reasons I maintain that Oscar has foresight. The long-term strategy, such as scalable operations, is the primary driver of the projected CAGR of revenue at 20% to 2027. So far, the company is engaging in these activities in expansion while offering disruptive products such as ICHRA. Now, when you add all that together, the company has kept a 20% revenue growth YoY commitment. The revenue in the last quarter rose by 23.21%, from $2,864 million in Q3’2024 to $2,986 million in Q3’2025.

So far, the company has reaffirmed 2025 revenue guidance of between $12.0 billion and $12.2 billion. This means that in terms of annual revenue growth, FY2025 is likely to increase by around 31.52%, which is within the consensus revenue estimate of $12.02 billion. With the expansion discussed above, which aims to have a total market reach of about 16 million subscribers from the expected 6 million subscribers between 2025 and 2027, the revenues are set to increase beyond 2025.

Even though the company is not yet profitable at the moment, with a trailing net loss of $244.09 million, it is expected, due to huge expenses on expansion commitments, to scale its revenue base. From FY2022, the company has demonstrated strong execution by reducing net loss from $606.3 million to a net profit of $25.4 million in 2024. With the expansion in 2025, which seems to have resulted in investing back its earnings, the earnings projection shows impressive possible returns, as shown by the upward consensus estimates. A good example of this investing back into the company is seen in the projected Q4’2025 earnings miss by around 47.72% YoY, followed by an optimistic Q2’2026 at 93.26% earnings rebound.

To wrap up this section, I will value Oscar Health using the P/S ratio compared to that of its competitors, Humana Inc. (NYSE:HUM) and Centene Corporation (NYSE:CNC). Oscar Health seems to be trading at a premium relative to its competitors at a P/S ratio of 0.37x, which is a distant from HUM’s P/S ratio of 0.23x and CNC’s at 0.11x. I think this is a justified market valuation given expansion efforts to capture the market through more enrollment. This is because while FY2026 faces significant subsidy uncertainty as discussed above, the company still has a revenue growth rate estimate of 4.77%, compared to HUM, which is -27.56%. CNS may seem to have a high revenue growth rebound in 2026 of 46.33%, but it is after a -72.14% projected revenue drop in 2025. Therefore, Oscar Health is relatively stable compared to its peers following expansion commitments, new products such as ICHRA that are already commercialised, and affordable policy charges.

Investment Risks

Regulatory and Policy Uncertainty

Oscar Health faces a heavy risk of ACA subsidy uncertainties. The company is subject to federal regulations, such as potential subsidies modification or expiration post 31st December 2025, which could materially impact membership growth and revenue. The ACA expansion exposes the company to a shifting regulatory and political environment. For example, if subsidies are reduced or withdrawn, the affordability of Oscar’s policy charges may erode and may affect enrollment and profitability. Possible increases without subsidies already indicate a significant increase in insurance policies, which might result in possible membership attrition.

Medical Cost Volatility and Margin Pressure

Right now, when the subsidies are still effective, the medical loss ratio as of Q3’2025 is at 88.5%, from 84.6% in 2024. Following FY2025's reaffirmed guidance of a medical loss ratio ranging between 86.0% and 87.0%, it indicates that the company is still facing cost pressure. With the modifications or withdrawal of subsidies as explained above, the company is likely to encounter sustained cost pressures. As such, more dollars may need to be allocated to cover medical expenses. This may eventually result in a challenge in meeting financial forecasts.

Conclusion

I am rerating OSCR from a hold to a buy, a bullish stance on Oscar Health, driven by ACA expansion enrollment to new areas. I believe that this fundamental optimism, especially in regions with high enrollment scores, is a significant step forward to consolidate more members, which has a direct impact on revenues.

data.title: Ether's Out Of Favor - Why I'm Buying ETH Again Anyway
data.description:
Ether has disappointed investors in 2025, particularly those who climbed aboard over the summer, when the cryptocurrency soared to near $5000. That’s also when Tom Lee of Fundstrat argued for much higher ether prices, which investors could gain exposure to through this Digital Asset Treasury Fund Bitmine Immersion (BMNR).

I had a buy rating on the Grayscale Ethereum Mini Trust ETF (ETH) back in August. That turned out to be a lousy call, with ether falling below what I deemed “critical support” at the $4100 level. From there, the token plunged to near $2600... a support spot I detailed just a few weeks ago.

So, I’m keeping with a buy rating on ETH. In fact, after taking a tax loss in November on my ether position, I plan to buy back in next week. My trade was selling an ETH ETF and buying the iShares Bitcoin ETF (IBIT). Catching my eye right now is a relative chart of ether to bitcoin. Ether shows relative strength for the first time since August, and I see that as a telltale sign that the entire cryptocurrency market’s bearish run lately may, in fact, be over.

ETH -6% YTD

According to the issuer, ETH is solely and passively invested in ether. Its investment objective is to reflect the value of ether held by the Fund, less expenses and other liabilities. Ether is a digital asset that is created and transmitted through the operations of the peer-to-peer Ethereum Network, a decentralized network of computers that operates on cryptographic protocols. The Ethereum Network allows people to exchange tokens of value, called ether, which are recorded on a public transaction ledger known as a blockchain.

ETH is a small ETF with $2.3 billion in assets under management as of December 4, 2025. That’s down from $2.9 billion at the time of my previous analysis. The annual expense ratio is low at just 15 basis points, while there is no yield. Low cost and no distributions make ETH ideal for taxable brokerage accounts (not IRAs or HSAs).

Share-price momentum has turned dreadful, now featuring a weak D- ETF Grade by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. For perspective, it was an A+ just three months ago. But risk happens fast in the crypto space, and with a high historical standard deviation, ETH scores very poorly on risk metrics. Hence, my ether ETF stake will be only about a 0.5% portfolio weight (IBIT is 1.5% of my overall net worth).

Finally, on liquidity, ETHA is highly tradable. Its average daily volume is north of 6 million shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at just two basis points, per Grayscale.

So, key to my bullish thesis is relative price action between ether and bitcoin. I see a breakout underway, in which the often more speculative ether is jumping to multi-week highs versus bitcoin. The months-long correction with both coins may be over. And I’m putting my money where my writing is—I'll buy ETH early next week once my 30-day wash-sale period is over.

ETHUSD vs BTCUSD: Breakout

I encourage readers to check out my BMNR article from last month. I make the case that while Tom Lee’s product has its appeals, I’d rather own ether outright through a low-cost ETF.

Tom Lee's Bold Ether Call

Seasonally, there are tailwinds. Historically, gains have been strong from December through May, while volatility has been the theme from June through November. I like to say that seasonality is secondary to price, but this is a bullish factor to consider.

ETHUSD: Bullish December-May Trends

Finally, ether’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that the token held key support at the $2600 mark, while a bearish death cross indeed occurred (it’s something I called out would be likely to happen in my BMNR article). But take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart—it is just now breaking out of a downtrend. They say that momentum tends to turn before price, so this could be a bullish harbinger for ETH price action to come.

Still, there remains a significant amount of volume by price up to the $4100 mark, and that level is major long-term resistance following the bearish false breakout in August. For now, technicals are not outright strong, but there are bullish early indicators.

ETHUSD: RSI Breakout, Death Cross a Risk, $2600 Support Held

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on ETH. I plan to buy the ETF next week, as its relative strength to IBIT is at fresh multi-week highs, while ether itself has held long-term support. Bullish calendar trends are also underway.

data.title: Well-Earned Mean-Reversion Continues In Real Estate
data.description:
From 1975 through the early 2000s, Canadian median home prices rose broadly in line with median inflation-adjusted household income. Where home prices outpaced income gains in the late 1970s and 1980s, the disconnect was short-lived before prices corrected more than 30%.

From 2000 to 2022, Canadian home prices (shown in red below through Q2 2021) increased by 375% - 46% of that from 2020-22 - while the average Canadian wage rose 3% annually (disposable income in black).

My own real estate holdings were leaping along for the ride. Still, it was easy to see that this was not good for financial stability, and I was writing about the horror of it all in real-time.

In the Greater Toronto and Vancouver Areas, where most of the population lives, median home prices rose 450% and 490%, respectively, from 2000 through 2022.

The Greater Toronto Area (GTA), which includes five regions (mapped below), is the largest population centre in Canada by a wide margin - home to some 7 million Canadians (approximately 17% of the national population).

The area has the most diversified economy in Canada (finance, tech, logistics, healthcare, education, and manufacturing), has traditionally received the most immigrants annually of any Canadian region, and has a median after-tax household income of $97,000, some 15% higher than the national average of $84,000 (StatsCan).

Canada’s price-boosting monetary and fiscal policies (a decade of ultra-low interest rates, easy lending, quantitative easing by central banks, minimal down payments, government underwriting of mortgages, weak money laundering rules, and an unlimited principal residence tax exemption) enabled home prices in the GTA (and many areas within an hour’s drive of it) to a decade-long 2.74x leap in the average sale price from $467,300 in 2012 to $1,281,900 by peak mania in February 2022 - a nominal annualized increase of about 10.6%.

The latest update from the Toronto Regional Real Estate Board (TRREB) shows that prices dropped sharply in November to an average sale price of $951,700, and have now declined 25.8% (-$330k) in nominal terms since the 2022 peak.

Despite the substantial pullback (as shown below since 2007), this only puts prices back to January 2021 levels - speaking volumes about the speculative surge that took place during the low-rate investor frenzy.

The GTA’s median after-tax household income of $97,000 is about 15% higher than Canada’s national figure of $84,000, meaning the average GTA home sale price was more than 13x the median household income at the 2022 peak and is still a mind-blowing 10x now.

Nationally, the figures are not much better. Despite an 18% nominal and 25% real decline in average sale prices since February 2022 (shown below since 1970), the average home sale price of $690,195 in October was still some 8.2x the median household income and just back to 2021 levels.

If home prices are to realign with historic affordability norms of no more than 3x household income, then nationally, Canadian home prices would need to halve from here, or household incomes would need to nearly triple. Perhaps we can hope for some combination of lower prices and higher incomes, but the catalysts for a big boost to household income are not presently apparent.

In the meantime, active listings (in blue below since 2010) are far outpacing sales (in grey), along with record listing cancellations, as sellers pray for a return of price-insensitive buyers in the spring.

November is typically not a big month for home buying in the region, but it was still the third-weakest in at least 15 years.

Anecdotal evidence from agents suggests some sellers are waiting for the Spring, with some even de-listing and hoping easing financing conditions help manage demand—a bold move considering the overall inventory trend.

Weak sales and a weaker—but still strong—inflow of inventory pushed total inventory much higher. The board saw 24,549 active listings last month, soaring 16.8% higher from last year. This is unprecedented for November, with the above chart really emphasizing how this market is a totally different beast.

November and December typically aren’t big months for Greater Toronto real estate. Consequently, weak sales aren’t the big red flag they present in busier Spring months. However, winter inventory may have a big impact as it piles up. It sets the stage for a Spring market that sellers are reportedly holding out for—just as a wave of investor-owned new construction completions are expected to hit.

An ongoing downturn in home prices is the cure for too-high prices. Still, it bears noting that housing corrections have traditionally led to the worst economic downturns, with painful losses in employment and the stock market. Unfortunately, we’ve earned a doozy.

Disclosure: No positions

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: American Homes 4 Rent: Low Risk, High Return
data.description:
2025 has been a rough year for residential REITs and American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) has been no exception, with shares down 17% year-to-date. With AMH shares down while the broader market has steadily increased, one might suspect some sort of business implosion, or at the very least, missed guidance. But the reality is that while the share price is down, the business of renting single-family homes is performing just fine. Earlier this month, AMH reiterated guidance for 2025 same-store NOI guidance ahead of its initial 2025 midpoint guide (4.0% vs. 3.25%).

I believe the disconnect between the performance of the business and the performance of the stock has created an attractive opportunity for conservative, long-term investors. Despite having a steady cash flow stream, a favorable long-term outlook, and a rock-solid balance sheet, AMH trades at its lowest valuation multiples since coming public.

With a 3-5 year investment horizon, I expect AMH to generate mid-teens annualized returns. Given what I see as a very low fundamental risk profile, I consider this to be a very attractive return and have been aggressively adding shares to my portfolio.

Recent Results / Medium Term Outlook

As shown above, AMH recently confirmed that it expects to achieve 4% same-store NOI growth for 2025. 4% is consistent with the midpoint of guidance given at the time AMH released 3Q25 earnings and is +0.75% higher than the midpoint of initial guidance given back in February. Strength in results is underpinned by high resident retention rates coupled with 4+% renewal rent growth.

While all residential housing REITs have seen sustained increases in resident retention, SFR (single-family rental) retention has been 75-80% (above the high 50s for apartment REITs) for both AMH and peer Invitation Homes (INVH). Retention remains high given that renting is much more affordable than owning a home given the 28% increase in median US home prices since 2019 (pre-pandemic), a near doubling of mortgage rates, as well as continued increases in property taxes, maintenance, and insurance. As you can see below, renting has become a much more attractive alternative to owning over the past 15 years.

Additionally, as shown above, the outlook for demand over the long term remains promising, with above average population growth expected for AMH's key demographic of 35-49-year-olds. There is a large pipeline of millennials who are expected to marry, have kids, and move to single-family homes - given the affordability challenges, renting will prove to be the best (and in many cases the only) option available to these future households. This bodes well for AMH and should support continued strong rent growth into the future.

Valuation

At today's $31 share price, AMH trades at an implied cap rate of 6.3% (2026e), 15.7x FFO (2026e), and $281,000 per home. On a cap rate and P/FFO basis, this is as cheap as AMH has been since its 2013 IPO. Historical valuations over the past dozen years have ranged from 4.0%-6.3% on an implied cap rate basis and 15.7-28.2x FFO. AMH is slightly more expensive than its peer Invitation Homes (which I recently wrote up here on Seeking Alpha), which trades at a 6.4% implied cap rate (2026e) and 14x FFO (2026e).

Assuming a 3.8% same-store NOI CAGR between now and 2030 and a return to a 5% implied cap rate (near midpoint of the historical range), I derive a 2030 price target of $50 per share. Factoring in the nearly 4% dividend yield, this works out to just over a 15% annualized return.

Moreover, despite the attractive expected return, investors are taking very little fundamental risk in buying AMH shares. The balance sheet is very conservatively managed with net debt to EBITDA of 5.1x. This works out to an LTV (loan to value) of just under 25%. Imagine buying a home with 75% down!

Risks

REITs in general and residential REITs like AMH are deeply out of favor as investors chase the tech sector/AI. Shares could remain out of favor.

A rise in interest rates could cause interest expenses to rise and reduce the multiple (increase the cap rate) investors are willing to pay for real estate. Approximately 20% of AMH's debt will mature over the next 3 years. Should interest rates rise 100 basis points, I estimate this would reduce FFO by $0.06, or ~3%.

Should AI adoption lead to a sharp increase in unemployment, this would reduce household formation (demand) and could reduce NOI growth or even cause NOI to decline. Were this to occur, I expect the Fed would meaningfully cut interest rates, which could increase the multiple investors are willing to pay for real estate (cap rate compression).

Conclusion

Given my expectation for a mid teens annualized returns over the medium term, coupled with below average risk profile, I see AMH has a very attractive investment opportunity and have purchased a meaningful position in the stock.

data.title: Eaton: Growth Is Largely Priced In
data.description:
Shares of Eaton (ETN) have been a disappointing performer over the past year, losing about 9% of their value. While its core business is doing well, there is already substantial optimism priced into shares. When I last covered shares in August, I rated Eaton a hold. While I felt valuation was “getting stretched” and pricing in a “fairly perfect outcome,” I felt the momentum of the AI boom was too strong to sell shares. With the stock down 4% even as the market has gained 6%, my valuation concerns were reasonable and arguably should have driven a sell recommendation. After this modest pullback, now is a good time to see if underperformance will continue or if shares can rebound as we head into 2026.

Looking first at recent results, in the company’s third quarter reported on November 4th, Eaton earned $2.59, which was $0.15 below expectations. While revenue grew by 10% to $7 billion, this was also $90 million below consensus. Back in August, I wrote ETN would need to deliver beats and raises to continue the rally in shares, and this past quarter fell short of that, even as ETN maintained full-year guidance, suggesting the misses were entirely timing-based, and organic sales growth was 7%.

To be clear, recent results were not weak; rather, ETN has been a victim of very high expectations. On the positive side, its book-to-bill ratio was 1.1x for the 12 months ending in Q3 as the company built more backlog. Keeping this ratio above 1x is essential to maintain revenue growth into the future and speaks to the strong demand ETN has across most end markets. Electrical Americas orders surged to $4 billion in Q3. Its electrical backlog is now 4x larger than its historic level.

At a segment level, Electrical Americas revenue was up 15% to $3.4 billion, and margins expanded 20 bps to 30.3%. Its backlog is now $12 billion. Its Electric Global unit grew revenue 10% to $1.7 billion, with margins also expanding 40 bps to 19.1%. Data center spending is disproportionately focused in the US, which is why its US segment has faster growth.

Aerospace continues to gain momentum, reaching a record with sales up 14% to $1.08 billion. Margins also were up an excellent 150 bps to 25.9%. Its backlog is up an equally strong 15%, and orders rose 11% in Q3. Despite challenges across the broader aerospace supply chain, ETN has managed this unit extremely well, gaining market share, and I see the outlook here as also being bright.

Vehicle sales fell by 8% to $639 million, and margins compressed 160 bps to 17.8%. Given tariffs and muted consumer demand, volumes have been challenged here. While we are likely nearing a bottom in my view, any recovery is likely to be slow, with stability a more reasonable target.

Looking forward over the near term, while speaking at a conference on December 2nd, Eaton affirmed it is “on track” to deliver guidance, which should alleviate concerns that Q3 pressures would persist. The company does see softness in the auto and residential construction markets. I expect Q4 results to come in at least at the midpoint of guidance given December commentary. Back in August, I was targeting $3.8-$4 billion of free cash flow this year. I still view this as the central case.

Shares offer a 1.2% dividend yield. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is also down 2.1% from last year. The company maintains an excellent balance sheet with less than $10 billion of debt. As such, Eaton is well positioned to continue returning capital to investors and doing bolt-on M&A, like its recent Boyd purchase, with its free cash flow.

Separately, in November, Eaton announced its CFO would be leaving in April 2026 to enable an orderly transition. The company will be searching for a successor both internally and externally, and I like to see a replacement announced by February to provide some overlap and continuity. I do not expect a change in financial policy as a result, and this orderly transition does not alter my outlook for shares.

More broadly, at the December conference, management discussed 2026, saying it expects its end market to grow about 7% and anticipates outperforming this. With incremental margins of ~30%, this implies 10-12% operating income growth next year. With some share repurchases, this leaves me expecting $13.60-$14 of EPS next year and $4.4-$4.5 billion of free cash flow. The company is enjoying secular tailwinds that support this growth.

Namely, as you can see below, technology capex spending has surged in recent years, with large tech firms set to basically triple their 2024 spending by 2027. Under their current plans, we should see spending rise over 30% next year vs. their year. Increased data center spending is a significant tailwind for Eaton’s business and has become a driving source of its growth. I expect orders and revenue to grow at least for several years.

I do remain cautious on whether all anticipated spending occurs. Aside from long-term views on AI, there are finite amounts of construction crews, supply chains for materials, etc., which may simply make it difficult to build out data centers as quickly as each company hopes to. While all of this spending may occur, there is a risk it takes longer to build out facilities. Additionally, as companies like Oracle (ORCL) and OpenAI become increasingly reliant on external financing to meet buildout plans, that adds another layer of risk to long-term growth. Overall, even if we undershoot plans beyond 2026, Eaton is likely to benefit from rising data center demand for years. But given capacity constraints, the pace of growth is unlikely to accelerate, and if anything, it should steadily slow.

Elsewhere, Eaton’s aerospace unit should continue to benefit from the turnaround at Boeing (BA), which appears to be gaining traction. As Boeing’s production continues to improve, that should filter through to more orders and revenue for Eaton, providing an additional leg of growth for the company.

On the other hand, I am cautious about Eaton’s outlook for slight growth in the residential market. As you can see below, the number of homes under construction has been steadily declining. With the levels of starts muted, we are likely to see declines continue naturally at least into mid-2026 before bottoming. I believe we are approaching a bottom in residential construction but are not quite there yet, so ETN’s prediction may prove a little early. Still, this is a small piece of the business, and assuming the ~3% decline I expect, this would be a sub-0.5% impact on Eaton’s overall growth prospects.

Still, the outlook is bright, but much is priced into shares. Eaton should generate ~$11.75-$12 free cash flow in 2025, giving shares a ~3.5% free cash flow yield. The stock is also trading at ~24x 2026 earnings. With upper single-digit growth, ETN is well positioned to grow into this multiple over time and remains a lower beta way to play the tech buildout. That said, I believe it is too early to start buying shares, especially after an earnings miss in its most recent quarter. I continue to view $325 as a buy point, and if shares were to pull back there, I would be a buyer. For now, I remain a “hold” and recommend patience before adding.

data.title: The S&P USD Select Leveraged Loan Index: A Comprehensive Approach To The Leveraged Loan Market
data.description:
By Eric Pettinelli

The S&P USD Select Leveraged Loan Index is a new entrant in the leveraged loan landscape, developed to fill a critical gap in existing liquid leveraged loan index offerings that typically focus on the largest loans by market capitalization. This innovative index encompasses all USD-denominated fully funded term loans with a minimum facility size of USD 500 million and credit ratings below investment grade, providing a broader view of the market.

As the U.S. leveraged loan market surged to an estimated USD 1.5 trillion in July 2025, the diversity of borrowers expanded significantly, incorporating a wide range of loan types, credit qualities, spreads, covenants and other characteristics. This variety is often not fully captured when analyzing only the top 100 largest loans in the market. The back-tested performance of the index was positive over the last five years, as showcased in Exhibit 1.

This index not only provides a more holistic and representative view of the liquid leveraged loan market by including loans of USD 500 million and above, but also enhances sector diversification and risk/performance profile with a total of 930 constituents ending October 2025.

Examining the credit quality characteristics of loans beyond the top 100 by notional index value offers deeper insights into the broader market. These insights include tracking defaults of underlying loans, which serve as crucial early warning signs in the leveraged loan ecosystem—information that may be missed when focusing solely on the largest loans.

Additionally, potential risks such as credit deterioration become more evident with a larger dataset, allowing investors and market observers to make more informed decisions. The index also incorporates higher spreads into the overall mix, driven by the inclusion of smaller loans and lower average credit quality. Exhibit 2 illustrates the difference in credit quality between the top 100 loans in the S&P USD Select Leveraged Loan Index and the rest of the constituents as of October 2025.

The effects of broader loan inclusion also extend to sector profile, as the addition of lower notional value loans provides access to a wider array of business types within the index, as shown in Exhibit 3.1 For an index that focuses on including lower credit quality borrowers, diversifying across industries can have a positive effect, not only for encompassing additional sources of performance but also for moderating risk.

In summary, the S&P USD Select Leveraged Loan Index offers a benchmark for measuring larger loans within the leveraged loan universe without sacrificing the risk/performance balance and diversification qualities that result from the additional inclusion of smaller issuances. As the leveraged loan market continues to evolve and expand, indices that adopt a comprehensive view of the leveraged loan market will serve as essential tools for more informed investment decisions.

1 The S&P USD Select Leveraged Loans Index follows the iBoxx Industry Classification Standard (iBICs). For further details, please see iBICs | S&P Dow Jones Indices

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: IEV: European Stocks Remain Attractively Valued Going Into 2026
data.description:
Introduction

After a decade of underperformance relative to U.S. stocks, most recently driven by the Russia-Ukraine war, European equities have had a strong 2025, benefiting from dollar weakness and attractive valuations (discussed in my prior coverage). In numbers, the iShares Europe ETF (IEV) lagged the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) by around 181% over the past ten years, but has managed to outperform by roughly 14% so far in 2025, delivering a gain of ~32%:

Looking ahead to 2026, I believe IEV should continue to outperform VOO, confirming my Buy rating on the ETF. My bullish outlook is based on:

  • An attractive trailing earnings yield of 5.46% for IEV, well above that of the S&P 500-tracking VOO.
  • A total return outlook of 7.9% achieved with conservative 1% GDP growth for countries where IEV invests.
  • A well-covered current dividend of 2.22%, roughly double that of the S&P 500.

In this article, I will expand on these points and discuss risks related to the IEV investment case.

ETF Overview

You can access all IEV details on the iShares website here. The iShares Europe ETF invests in large-cap European stocks, with its benchmark index being the S&P Europe 350. The ETF's largest allocation is to the United Kingdom (23.45% of the fund's assets), followed by France (16.33%), Switzerland (14.38%), and Germany (14.19%):

Relative to my prior coverage, allocation to the top four countries has decreased across the board, offset by larger allocations to the Netherlands (7.4% of ETF assets), Spain (5.75%), and Italy (5.05%).

From a sector perspective, IEV's main exposure is to financials (24.23% of ETF assets), followed by industrials (18.22%), and health care (14.07%):

Relative to my prior coverage, allocation to cyclical sectors such as financials, industrials, and consumer discretionary has increased at the expense of defensive sectors such as consumer staples and health care, indicating a slight increase in the ETF's already pronounced cyclical tilt.

The ETF's expense ratio stands at 0.61%, well above low-cost ETFs, which charge 0.1-0.2% in expense fees. As such, I view IEV as more of a short-term play on attractive European valuations, which I will discuss below, rather than a solid long-term holding for passive investors.

Current Valuation

Given the overweight position in cyclical sectors outlined above, it is no surprise that valuations for IEV are quite low. Indeed, IEV holdings trade at roughly 18.3x their trailing earnings, or an earnings yield of about 5.46%.

While this remains notably cheaper than U.S. large caps, which trade at 28.15x their trailing earnings, IEV is not as cheap as it was when I last covered the ETF at the start of 2025. Indeed back then IEV offered a P/E of only 15.27x, representing an earnings yield of about 6.5%.

In essence, strong recent returns for European stocks have diminished their long-term return potential. That said, European companies remain notably cheaper than their U.S. counterparts.

Dividend remains attractive

Cheaper valuations are reflected in IEV's generous dividend yield of 2.22% (almost double that of the S&P 500-tracking VOO), although growth over the past decade, at only 2.41%, reflects the poor returns shown by European large-caps over the period:

Looking ahead, taking into account the elevated expense ratio of 0.61% and current valuations, I calculate IEV can reasonably grow its dividends by about 5.7% over the medium term, which results in an overall attractive 7.9% total return potential for the ETF. This is arrived at by assuming conservative 1% GDP growth for Europe in the long term.

IEV Rating

Heading into 2026, the buy case for IEV is clearly not as strong as it was at the start of 2025. The silver lining is that this is true for U.S. markets as well. Truth is stocks cannot fundamentally deliver the type of double-digit returns we have had in 2025 year in, year out without significant pullbacks.

As such, if you are happy to accept a lower total return potential, perhaps with a somewhat elevated risk of a pullback, I still believe that IEV represents a solid holding over the near to medium term. Indeed, while the war in Ukraine is still ongoing, I remain optimistic that a diplomatic solution will be found sooner or later. This may provide another catalyst for European equities.

All in all, while I am not as bullish on IEV as I was at the start of 2025, I confirm my Buy rating on the ETF as I think it will outperform the S&P 500-tracking VOO in 2026.

Risks

Given the overweight position in cyclical sectors exhibited by IEV, a downside surprise to European GDP growth is a key risk in the investment case. Looking at the IMF's 2026 growth expectations, we see that 2026 GDP growth in key IEV holdings such as the UK (+1.3%), France (+0.9%), Germany (+0.9%), and Switzerland (+1.3%) would be largely consistent with my circa 1% long-term GDP growth outlook, with marginally stronger growth coming from small IEV positions such as the Netherlands (+1.2%) and Spain (+2%):

The other key development for European equities will be a potential resolution to the war in Ukraine. Of course, there is also the risk of further escalation in fighting, which could spark a spike in energy prices - highly negative for the European economy, which remains a net energy importer.

I would also mention that a reversal of recent USD weakness is a specific risk for U.S. investors, as the ETF is traded in USD, but its value depends on the prices of European companies traded in local currencies such as GBP and EUR.

In the longer term, as discussed in the ETF Overview section, investors in IEV may want to shift some of their allocation to cheaper European ETF alternatives, or consider investing in select European companies directly, thus saving on the quite elevated 0.61% expense fee.

Conclusion

After a decade of poor returns leading up to 2025, this year has finally signaled a turning point for European equities. While the bulk of the 14% outperformance relative to the S&P 500 stems from currency effects, under the hood European stocks remain much cheaper relative to their U.S. peers, likely indicating another year of outperformance in 2026.

European stocks are clearly not as cheap as they were at the start of 2025. As such, I believe investors should moderate their return expectations over the medium term, which I realistically see at about 7.9% in local currency terms.

I confirm a Buy rating on IEV heading into 2026. That said, the ETF's high expense ratio may warrant a review of their position by some investors, either looking for cheaper ETF alternatives or investing directly in European companies.

Key risks in the investment case include European GDP growth falling short of expectations in 2026, a reversal of recent USD weakness, as well as the ongoing war in Ukraine.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Bonterra Energy: Winter To The Rescue
data.description:
Overshadowing the third quarter report is the weather. A polar vortex event promises some very cold temperatures for at least another week (and maybe two weeks) in December. For a company like Bonterra Energy (OTCPK:BNEFF) the weather is providing a lot of badly needed good news for a company that still has considerable natural gas production. It is also providing a respite (however temporary) from all the worries marked by threats of trade wars and tariffs. North America is also adding a fair amount of export capacity that should tighten natural gas supplies as well. All of this could help the company reset the balance sheet at a time when such a reset would provide some liquidity to continue to shop for premium acreage.

Trading

Before going any further, it needs to be stated that the founder and major shareholder is George Fink who is now Chairman Emeritus. In a situation like this maybe half the shares are available for trading at any given time. This will affect volume and liquidity for this small company.

Because of the volume and liquidity situation, it is best to be patient and use limit orders. Liquidity may be better if your brokers allow purchases and sales in Canada. But with any small company, this one in particular, there has got to be some patience, or you can pay a lot more for shares than you need to.

Last Article

This was yet another natural gas producer that was looking for a more profitable production mix which would also enable the company to thrive under a wide variety of scenarios. The last article covered at least part of the transition. More acreage acquired was covered in earlier articles.

As a result of these moves, the company now needs less capital to either sustain or grow production. But because the wells are larger, there can be considerable production variance by quarter when those larger wells experience that first year decline.

What has been happening as a result is summarized above. Production has grown with less wells brought online and of course lower capital costs. This is yet another way to build free cash flow faster than the company growth rate. It is also lowering the company breakeven point because the later wells obviously have a much lower breakeven point than existing production had.

That said, production has been declining all year because the few wells brought online were brought online early in the fiscal year. For wells with significant natural gas production, the exposure to the strong natural gas prices that exist during the heating season likely raise the return on investment a percentage point or two. So, it makes sense to not bring wells online in the summer.

Now as some of this acreage produces a greater percentage of liquids production, that consideration may not apply in the future to at least some wells drilled.

Revised Guidance

The revised guidance was looking more and more optimistic from a pricing viewpoint because it used WTI $70 as the price of oil. But what is happening now is that the natural gas price of C$2.25 may turn out to be far too conservative if that polar vortex causes a lot of natural gas to be used due to some abnormally cold temperatures.

Keep in mind that this is a Canadian producer that reports using Canadian dollars unless noted. WTI is a United States dollar denominated price for oil.

The exposure to natural gas is fairly significant at roughly half the production. So, this could get interesting. La Ninas are known for weak polar vortexes that can leak cold air south. So, what is happening now is very much an expectation of La Nina (but by no means guaranteed). These cold air leaks are what give La Ninas their cold reputation. Without these leaks (that happen for various reasons) La Ninas are actually warm.

But nothing helps like revising natural gas production upward as shown above right in time for unusually cold weather that can push natural gas prices sky high.

Debt

A big issue here was the refinance of debt before lower commodity prices would have increased the challenge. This wiped out all the debt that was coming due within the next two years. Now the company has some breathing room and some liquidity.

Note that the new notes take the debt out of the hands of banks (which happens to be a lot more typical of small companies anywhere) and puts them into bonds or notes. This tends to be a much more flexible arrangement.

Banks are notorious for worrying about loans at the bottom of the business cycle or when a recession hits. Too much debt with a bank can destabilize a company. This is likely the reason that some of the bank debt went into the notes that were issued as well.

The reason for the stock repurchase plan is not only is the stock seen as cheap, but also such a plan is seen as more flexible than paying a dividend. Right now, the weak commodity prices have been raising the debt ratio.

We saw before how management was increasing free cash flow. But now a polar vortex event could well strengthen natural gas prices that would send the debt ratio back down while providing a fair amount of extra cash to retire some debt. Waiting to see how the fourth quarter turns out is a very conservative way to operate. It is also smart because the weather is notoriously low visibility. But the stock buyback plan provides shareholder returns until management feels secure enough to begin a dividend.

Summary

Events that are largely outside of management's control are threatening to overwhelm or dominate the company's prospects for some time to come. The company has had decent cash flow all year and the debt ratio is still below 1.5. But now a polar vortex may make the fourth quarter into the most significant quarter for the company in some time. Even if the company pays nothing on its debt, that debt ratio may well come down when the fourth quarter becomes part of the calculation.

In the meantime, the company is migrating to a more profitable production mix that includes liquids. That has led to a lower capital budget combined with a buildup of free cash flow.

The whole process makes this a continuing speculative strong buy idea. If you go back to my older articles, you will see that this company relied upon an income model too long. That nearly cost the company dearly. The turnaround from that situation is now well underway. This is going to be a different company going forward than the one that paid a consistent dividend in the past.

Fiscal year 2020 put the final nail in the coffin for upstream companies that tried to pay a dividend "no matter what". Much of the industry is now moving to a base dividend combined with stock repurchases so that the dividend paid is more defensible. But that means that share buybacks begin first.

Risks

The weather is very low visibility going forward and it is also fast moving. There is a long way for this La Nina winter to go. But right now, it sure is off to a good start for natural gas producers. Let us hope it keeps going this way.

Any turnaround like this one can be unsuccessful in the future for any number of unforeseen reasons. This turnaround story actually looks like it will get a short-term boost from the weather. But that can quickly change to become a setback.

So far, the debt levels have held steady. Cash burn appears about to reverse in a big way. Time will tell if that happens and if it is sustained as management has planned.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
